:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. In New Hampshire in 2020, people in prison for new court commitments accounted for 63% of the prison population, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 37%.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Who's in Prison?

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethinicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In New Hampshire, sentences have a minimum and a maximum term. However, if a sentence has a maximum of over one year (i.e. a felony sentence), the judge must also impose a 'disciplinary period' of incarceration of 150 days per year of the minimum term that can then be reduced through credit for good conduct. For example, a three to eight year sentence would have a 450 day 'disciplinary period' added on to the minimum term. If a sentence of imprisonment is imposed, the maximum term may not exceed: life for second-degree murder, 15 years for a class A felony, seven years for a class B felony, or one year for a class A misdemeanor. Some crimes carry mandatory minimum sentences. For example, a second or subsequent conviction for the felonious use of a firearm carries a mandatory minimum sentence of three years. A conviction under the state's version of the Armed Career Criminal Act carries a minimum sentence of ten years. Prisoners may be released on parole at the expiration of the minimum terms of their sentences, plus the disciplinary period of incarceration that must be imposed during sentencing, and minus any credit for good conduct. So, for example, if an individual is given a sentence of 10 to 25 years, they must serve their 10 year minimum and, in addition, 1,500 days (or about four years, one month, and 10 days) of a disciplinary period. However, through good time credit, the inmate can pare the 14 years plus that must be served before parole release eligibility back down to 10 years. Thus, the disciplinary period and good time are designed to offset each other.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 238 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new court commitments and with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 11% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 89% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new court commitments and with an original sentence length between 1 to 25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 89% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new court commitments and with an original sentence length between 1 to 25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 35-44 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 32% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new court commitments and with an original sentence length between 1 to 25 years.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new court commitments with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 2 to 4.9 years, accounting for 37% of this group.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new court commitments with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to rape or sexual assault, accounting for 23% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.
``` ::: :::
